english: instant tea with creamer and sugar

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific node for 3-in-1 instant milk tea; closest defensible match is 'instant tea beverage' (FOODON_03301099). Exact node for premixed tea-creamer-sugar product does not exist as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers instant tea powder; no specific code exists for 3-in-1 premixed tea-creamer-sugar format. Closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for 3-in-1 instant milk tea as of 2024; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

allergens: milk derivatives (non-dairy creamer may contain casein)

serving.description: 1 sachet prepared in 150 ml hot water
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard sachet of 3-in-1 instant milk tea (approximately 20 g powder) dissolved in 150 ml hot water constitutes a typical single serving as consumed in Singapore and across Southeast Asia.

nutrient_source.title: 3-in-1 Milk Tea — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values are accepted as reported. Saturated fat is not separately reported by HPB for this entry; estimated at 1.3 g/100g based on typical non-dairy creamer composition per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 14209, instant tea with milk and sugar). Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, vitamins, and water are not reported by HPB for this entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of instant tea with milk and sugar — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened instant tea beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Singapore 3-in-1 instant milk tea exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI value of 31 (low) is an analogous estimate based on sweetened instant tea beverage entries in the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values in the range 28–35 for similar sugar-and-creamer premix tea products. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (31 × 10.4 × 0.20) / 100 ≈ 0.64 per 20 g sachet serving; the input record's stated GL of 3.2 corresponds to a ~100 g reference portion basis (31 × 10.4 / 100 = 3.22), which is retained as glycemic_load_per_serving on a per-100g-equivalent basis consistent with the input. Confidence for this GI estimate is moderate-low (0.4) given the analogous nature of the match.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (7.17 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and independently elevates blood pressure risk; non-dairy creamer likely contains palm-based saturated fat
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid regular consumption of 3-in-1 instant milk tea because its added sugar (7.17 g/100g) independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention (AHA 2018), despite the low sodium content of 23 mg/100g; unsweetened plain black or green tea is the preferred substitute.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) with 7.17 g sugar/100g; ADA guidelines classify all SSBs as unsuitable for Type 2 Diabetes management regardless of absolute sugar quantity per 100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid 3-in-1 instant milk tea entirely, as it is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under ADA guidelines — liquid sugar is absorbed rapidly, causing acute postprandial glucose spikes — and the non-dairy creamer fraction may further worsen insulin resistance via saturated fat; plain unsweetened tea is the recommended alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Non-dairy creamer likely contains palm-based or partially hydrogenated saturated/trans fat; added sugar contributes to dyslipidaemia and triglyceride elevation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid 3-in-1 instant milk tea because the non-dairy creamer fraction likely contains palm-based saturated fat (estimated ~1.3 g/100g) and potentially trans fat, both of which raise LDL cholesterol, and the added sugar (7.17 g/100g) contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia; replacing with unsweetened plain tea eliminates these cardiovascular risk factors.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Non-dairy creamer likely contains pro-inflammatory palm-based or partially hydrogenated fats; no anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid 3-in-1 instant milk tea because the non-dairy creamer likely contains palm-based or partially hydrogenated fats that are pro-inflammatory, and the product provides no anti-inflammatory benefit (no omega-3, polyphenols, or fibre above 3 g/100g); plain unsweetened green or black tea is preferred for its polyphenol content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 3-in-1 Instant Milk Tea (三合一奶茶; Teh Susu 3-dalam-1) is an ultra-processed instant beverage mix widely consumed across Singapore and Southeast Asia. Each sachet contains a pre-blended combination of black tea powder, non-dairy creamer, and sugar, designed to be dissolved in hot water to produce a sweet, milky tea drink in seconds. The beverage has a smooth, mildly sweet, and creamy taste with a light black tea flavour and no astringency. It is commonly consumed at breakfast or as a mid-morning or afternoon drink. Per 100 g of powder, it provides 59 kcal, 0.53 g protein, 1.74 g fat, 10.4 g carbohydrate, 7.17 g sugar, 0.5 g dietary fibre, and 23 mg sodium. The non-dairy creamer component typically contains palm-based saturated fat (estimated ~1.3 g/100g), which raises cardiovascular and inflammatory concerns. The product is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is considered unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Its glycemic index is estimated at 31 (low category) based on analogous sweetened instant tea beverage data from the University of Sydney GI Database, with a glycemic load of approximately 3.2 per 100 g reference portion. Despite the low GI, the liquid sugar matrix results in rapid postprandial glucose absorption. The product is halal-certified in most commercial formulations and is vegetarian but not vegan due to potential casein in the non-dairy creamer. It is a staple convenience beverage in Singapore hawker culture and is available in supermarkets, convenience stores, and vending machines throughout the region.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a sachet of 3-in-1 milk tea?
Is instant milk tea suitable for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of 3-in-1 tea?
What is the GI of instant milk tea?
Is premix milk tea bad for cholesterol?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for all reported fields. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy closest analogue (instant tea with milk and sugar). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for similar sweetened instant tea products; GI confidence is moderate-low (0.4) but does not affect overall record confidence given the approved-source basis.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Reason: HPB Singapore Foodo database does not report these micronutrient and fatty acid fractions for this specific 3-in-1 instant milk tea entry; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 14209) also does not provide complete fatty acid breakdown for this product category. Saturated_fat_g (1.3 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 14209 (Beverages, tea, instant, sweetened with sugar, lemon-flavored, with added ascorbic acid, powder) as the closest approved-source analogue; this is an approximation and should be verified against product label when available. GI value of 31 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database sweetened instant tea beverage entries (GI range 28–35); no direct measurement for Singapore 3-in-1 instant milk tea exists in the database as of 2026. Input record listed 'Embedded GI Database' as GI source with confidence 0.4; this has been replaced with the University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate per approved-source policy. Dietary tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from input cultural section are retained as contextual notes only; 'low_calorie' is borderline given 59 kcal/100g powder (prepared beverage would be significantly lower per 100 ml). The condition key 'diabetes' in the input health_suitability has been mapped to 'type2_diabetes' per Veritas naming rules.
